About Me

I think about people first,
then products.

My Psychology degree from Obafemi Awolowo University wasn't a side story — it's the thing that makes me different. I spent years being trained to notice what people don't say, to understand the gap between what someone asks for and what they actually need, and to read the dynamics in any room. This translates directly into how I approach research, how I write requirements, and how I show up in a stakeholder conversation.

Before I ever had the title "Product Manager," I was already doing the work — mapping user journeys, flagging friction nobody else had named, building tracking systems that turned scattered data into decisions, and coordinating delivery across engineering, design, and ops teams. The roles just didn't always carry the right label.

Product, for me, is where empathy meets execution. I care about understanding users deeply, but I care just as much about shipping something real — something that works, that lasts, and that moves a number that actually matters to the business.

SafeSignal — a safety incident reporting app I took from a blank page to a working prototype — is one example of how I think and work. But my curiosity isn't limited to one space. I'm drawn to products that sit at the intersection of people, data, and meaningful impact, wherever that intersection exists.

What makes me different

🧠

Psychology as a product skill

I don't just conduct user interviews — I know how to get past the surface answer to what's really driving behaviour. Most PMs learn this slowly. I started here.

🤝

The person engineers and designers actually want to work with

I translate between worlds without losing the thread. Engineers get clarity. Designers feel heard. Stakeholders get updates that make sense. Everyone moves faster.

⚡

I can build, not just spec

I work with AI tools, vibe-code prototypes, and no-code platforms to bring ideas to life quickly — before a single line of production code is written. In a world moving this fast, that's not a bonus. It's a requirement.

📊

Decisions, not just dashboards

I build tracking systems and define metrics because I want to know if something is working — not to have a slide to show. Data shapes what I do next, it doesn't just follow what I've already decided.

In my own words

Product is personal to me.

I chose this field because I genuinely believe good products change how people experience the world. Not in a grand abstract way — in the small, real moments where something works exactly the way it should, and the person using it doesn't have to think twice. That's what I'm building toward. Every time.

Skills

What I actually bring
to the table

These aren't things I've listed because they sound good. They're things I've used on real products, with real teams, to ship real work.

🔮

Discovery & User Research

I get to the real problem before anyone starts building. Through interviews, surveys, and insight synthesis, I separate what users say from what they actually mean — and turn that into a clear product direction.

User InterviewsJTBDPersonasUsability Testing
📋

PRDs & Requirements

I write docs that engineering teams actually use. Clear scope, well-defined user stories, acceptance criteria that leave no room for guesswork. Good documentation is a product skill — not just admin.

PRDsUser StoriesBDD/GherkinAcceptance Criteria
🗺

Roadmapping & Prioritisation

Knowing what to build is one skill. Knowing what to hold back — and making that case persuasively — is another. I do both, grounded in user evidence and business context.

MVP ScopingHypothesis TestingTrade-off Decisions
🤝

Stakeholder Management

I keep engineers, designers, ops leads, and leadership aligned without creating a calendar full of meetings. The right update at the right time, backed by data and told as a story — that's how trust gets built.

Power/Interest MappingRACI/DACICross-functional Comms
📊

Product Analytics

I define North Star metrics, build event tracking plans, and read funnels to understand where things are breaking down. Data shapes my next move — it doesn't just follow the one I've already made.

AARRRFunnelsNorth Star MetricEvent Tracking
🚀

Go-to-Market & Launch

I've planned launches end to end — from positioning and messaging to pilot programmes and success criteria. A great product that nobody hears about is still a failed product.

PositioningPilot PlanningLaunch Checklists

📱 Mobile App 🛡 Safety Tech 🇳🇬 Nigeria 2026 PM-built Prototype

SafeSignal —
Real-Time Safety Reporting App

From a blank page to a live, working prototype — designed, researched, scoped, and shipped by a PM who understands that great products are felt, not just used. This is what I do.

Explore Live Prototype →
<60s
Target report completion
100%
Anonymous by default
3
Insight-driven MVP decisions
5s
Report reaches safety team

The Product

A safety app built by a PM — not just spec'd by one.

SafeSignal is a mobile app that lets people in high-crime environments report incidents quickly, anonymously, and with enough evidence that safety teams can actually act on it. What makes this case study different isn't just what was built — it's that I built it. Not with an engineering team behind me, but by taking the product from zero to a fully interactive, tested prototype using AI tools, no-code platforms, and the same product thinking I'd bring to any real team. That's the kind of PM I am.

The Problem

People witness incidents and do nothing — not because they don't care, but because reporting feels risky, slow, and pointless.

Residents in high-crime areas face a broken system: traditional channels are intimidating, require too many steps, and offer no anonymity. Fear of exposure is the single biggest blocker. The result? Massive under-reporting, delayed responses, and communities feeling unsafe and unheard. When incidents go unreported, patterns stay invisible. Safety teams can't resource correctly. Trust in the systems meant to protect people quietly collapses.

Why It Mattered

This isn't just a UX problem. It's a public safety problem with a product-shaped solution.

Under-reporting doesn't just mean fewer records — it means safety coordinators are flying blind. They're deploying resources based on noise, not signal. The opportunity wasn't to build another reporting form. It was to make reporting feel safe, fast, and worth doing — so people actually do it.

What Changed

A reporting flow anyone can complete in under 60 seconds, from anywhere, with their identity fully protected.

The MVP focused on three things: speed under stress, anonymity by default, and evidence that safety teams can act on. I deliberately left out public maps, in-app chat, and offline mode — not because they don't matter, but because they weren't the question I needed to answer first. The question was: will people actually report? Build for that. Prove it. Then expand.

Screens from the live SafeSignal prototype — explore it yourself →

🔍
User Research Finding

The top blocker wasn't technology — it was trust. Once we knew that, anonymity stopped being a feature and became the product's core promise. Everything was built around it.

⚡
Key PM Decision

Public incident maps, in-app chat, and offline mode were all cut from the MVP — deliberately. I needed to answer one question first: will people report? Build for that. Prove it. Then expand.

🎯
Go-to-Market Strategy

The pilot targeted universities and community safety groups — not because they're the only users, but because they already had the trust infrastructure to make early adoption credible and measurable.

Experience

Every role taught me something
that has helped shape who I am today.

Before I ever had the title, I was already doing the work — shipping things, fixing things, figuring things out. Here's where it all happened.

📱

Product Manager — SafeSignal

TS Academy Capstone Project
February 2026

Took a safety reporting app from nothing to a live, working prototype — end to end. Led discovery research, wrote the PRD, defined the MVP, ran sprint planning, set up an analytics framework, and built the go-to-market plan. This wasn't a simulation — it was the real thing, done with real product thinking and shipped with real tools.

⚙

Project & Operations Manager

TILT – The Intentional Living Tribe (Remote)
February 2025 – Present

Worked alongside the engineering team to improve the platform — flagging usability issues nobody else had named, shaping content structure, and testing features before they went live. Built a data tracking system from scratch that turned scattered sales and feedback data into decisions the team could actually act on. Delivered 3+ online programmes per quarter, iterating the format every time based on what attendees told us.

🎯

Product & Content Coordinator

Valuemize Enterprise (Hybrid)
June 2020 – April 2022

Contributed directly to client-facing digital products — bringing user feedback into product decisions, collaborating with developers and designers on usability improvements, and managing a content team across multiple brands. Spotted a bottleneck in how work was being reviewed and approved, redesigned the process, and cut average turnaround time across projects.

📁

Operations & Administrative Coordinator

Bondbar Investment Limited
April 2016 – November 2018

Managed client communications, structured internal documentation, and prepared briefs for senior leadership. This is where I built the habits that still show up in how I work — clear communication, clean records, and never letting the detail fall through the cracks.
